summary: Learn how to approach, begin and end a bedside conversation respectfully.
objectives: Introduce yourself and obtain consent for a visit.
Notice whether the timing and environment are appropriate.
Respect a refusal without taking it personally.

sections.title: A simple shape for a visit
sections.body: Local signing-in and ward arrangements will be explained during your induction. The human part of the visit is consistent across placements.

items.title: Check in
items.body: Introduce yourself to ward staff, follow local instructions and ask whether anyone may particularly appreciate chaplaincy support.

items.title: Notice first
items.body: Is the patient asleep, receiving care, eating, on the telephone or talking privately? Do not interrupt clinical care or go behind closed curtains.

items.title: Introduce yourself
items.body: For example: “Hello, my name is Sam. I’m a volunteer with the chaplaincy team. Would you like a chat?”

items.title: Make the encounter comfortable
items.body: If invited to stay, use an appropriate chair. Do not sit on beds or clinical equipment. Consider privacy without isolating yourself unsafely.

items.title: End clearly
items.body: Summarise any agreed referral, check whether the patient needs anything passed on, say goodbye and complete the local sign-out process.

sections.label: Consent
sections.title: A welcome yesterday is not consent today
body: Consent is ongoing. A patient may be tired, in pain or simply not want company. A warm “Of course—take care” preserves dignity and leaves chaplaincy available for another time.

sections.label: At the bedside
sections.title: The relative answers for the patient
sections.prompt: Mark is unwell and his wife, Sue, answers every question for him. Mark is awake and appears to be listening. What could you do?

options.text: Continue speaking only to Sue because she clearly knows him best.
options.feedback: Sue’s perspective matters, but speaking only to her may unintentionally exclude Mark from his own encounter.

options.text: Acknowledge Sue, then gently address Mark directly and give him time to respond in whatever way he can.
options.feedback: This keeps the patient central while remaining respectful to his wife. If appropriate, either person may also value some private time to talk.

options.text: Ask Sue to leave immediately so that you can speak to Mark alone.
options.feedback: Private conversation may sometimes help, but it should be offered sensitively rather than imposed.

sections.debrief: Families are part of the situation, but the patient should not disappear from view simply because another person is more vocal.
